title: Memory Management and References

learningObjectives.text: Compare primitives versus objects using a new example and justify the result.
Explain stack and heap mental model using a new example and justify the result.
Explain references using a new example and justify the result.
Explain garbage collection using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns memory management and references from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **How JavaScript Works** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Primitives versus objects** → **Stack and heap mental model** → **References** → **Garbage collection**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Primitives versus objects
sections.body: Assigning a primitive copies its value. Assigning an object copies a reference to the same object, so mutations are visible through every reference.

sections.title: Stack and heap mental model
sections.body: Stack versus heap is a teaching model, not a language guarantee. Use it to reason about execution lifetime and managed objects, but focus on references, reachability, and value semantics.

sections.title: References
sections.body: Object variables hold references. Equality checks identity rather than structural similarity, and shallow copies may still share nested objects.

sections.title: Garbage collection
sections.body: Garbage collection reclaims values that are no longer reachable. Event listeners, timers, caches, and closures can accidentally retain references and extend lifetimes.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The outer object is copied but the nested reference is shared.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Memory Management and References connects Primitives versus objects, Stack and heap mental model, References.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Primitives versus objects” in the context of Memory Management and References?

choices.label: An execution context contains the information required to run a unit of code, including bindings, scope relationships, and a this binding. Function calls create additional contexts.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Primitives versus objects.

choices.label: Functions are first-class values: they can be stored, passed, and returned. Referring to a function is different from invoking it.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Primitives versus objects.

choices.label: A function can return a configured function that remembers creation-time context. This supports factories and reusable behavior.

questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Memory Management and References in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Apply Primitives versus objects deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.”?

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Memory Management and References?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Primitives versus objects, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Memory Management and References?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Use Memory Management and References in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

choices.label: Use Memory Management and References when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Avoid Memory Management and References completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Memory Management and References.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
